title: Resilience Ball-in-Basin Model
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive MicroSim demonstrating ecosystem resilience and regime shifts using the ball-in-basin metaphor with adjustable disturbance and resilience.

learningObjectives: Predict when a system will undergo a regime shift based on disturbance magnitude and resilience
Explain the ball-in-basin metaphor for ecosystem stability and tipping points
Analyze how gradual environmental degradation can make systems vulnerable to sudden collapse
Connect the resilience model to real-world examples of ecosystem regime shifts
prerequisites: Understanding of ecosystem stability and disturbance concepts
Familiarity with examples of ecosystem change

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Physics-based metaphor with interactive disturbance and degradation
keyQuestions: What does the depth of a basin represent in terms of ecosystem resilience?
Why is gradual degradation sometimes more dangerous than a single large disturbance?
Why is it harder to restore a degraded ecosystem than to degrade it?
What real-world factors erode ecosystem resilience over time?
commonMisconceptions: Ecosystems always return to their original state after a disturbance
Regime shifts happen suddenly without warning, when in fact resilience often erodes gradually
A degraded ecosystem can easily be restored by simply removing the disturbance
assessmentOpportunities: Students find the minimum disturbance threshold at different resilience levels
Students predict the outcome of slow degradation before pressing the button
Students draw their own ball-in-basin diagrams for real ecosystems
